text:
The Albanese government has announced almost $500 million in funding over two years for elite athletes ahead of the 2024 Paris Olympic Games.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has announced almost half a billion dollars in funding for elite athletes in the lead-up to the 2024 Paris Olympic Games.

Speaking at the Australian Institute of Sport on Friday, Mr Albanese detailed the government's investment strategy as it targets future success at the 2032 Brisbane Olympic Games.

The Albanese government has provided an additional $283 million on top of existing funding for elite sports.

“This $283 million in new money, on top of the annual funding that we give, will make a significant difference over the next two years,” Mr Albanese said.

The Prime Minister emphasised this represents a 50 per cent increase on the Morrison government’s 2021-22 high performance funding for the Tokyo Olympic Games and a “doubling” of paralympic athlete funding.

Including the $102 million in annual grant funding, the announcement will bring the Albanese government’s total high-performance investment to $489 million over two years.

The Prime Minister said he hoped the funding would “assist in Paris” for the Summer Olympic Games from July 26 to August 11 – and also prepare younger athletes for Brisbane 2032.

“The bottom line is I want to set up our youngest Australians for success at our own Olympic Games in Brisbane in 2032,” Mr Albanese said.

“We are a sporting nation and when our athletes are doing well, it lifts up our entire nation and inspires us.”

As well as providing a competitive edge, the Prime Minister promoted the benefits of participation in sport including improved mental wellbeing, lifestyle and social skills.

Mr Albanese said he hoped the investment would help get children off their phones and into the pool or onto the sporting field.

President of the Australian Olympic Committee Ian Chesterman welcomed the investment as “very good news” that would help athletes represent Australia on the international stage.

“This funding increase is a very important start to help our athletes be their best, inspire the generations to come and to build a strong sports system,” Mr Chesterman said.

President of Paralympics Australia Alison Creagh also praised the investment and declared it would “bring about monumental generational change for Para-sport”.
